turns.content: I'm trying to port my existing project and I'm stuck on how to handle test isolation in sqlx. My current approach just runs queries without wrapping them, so if one test inserts a row, the next test sees it even though they shouldn't share state.

That's exactly what causes flaky tests when you skip transaction boundaries. The solution is to use #[sqlx::test] which automatically creates an isolated database connection for each test function and rolls back the transaction after it finishes. This ensures every test starts with a clean slate.
Wait, does that mean I don't need to manually call begin_transaction or commit? Can I just remove all my manual DB setup code?

You can move away from manual transaction handling for basic tests, but you still need to define the test function with #[sqlx::test] and pass in your database connection. The macro handles wrapping each test call in its own isolated transaction automatically.
Okay so I just add #[sqlx::test] above my test functions? What about migrations though, how do those fit in?

Migrations run before your actual tests when you use the sqlx::test macro. The macro automatically executes pending migrations on the isolated connection first, then runs your test functions with fresh schema each time.
That's helpful but I'm still confused about how exactly the isolation works at the connection level versus transaction level. It feels like magic to me right now.

Think of it like this: each test gets its own private database instance for that test run, not just a transaction on shared data. The #[sqlx::test] macro creates a temporary connection pool specifically for your test suite, runs migrations on it once, then spins up fresh connections per test function so no test can see changes from another.
Ah okay, so if I delete my manual transaction code and just use #[sqlx::test], the tests will be isolated automatically without me writing rollback logic?

Exactly. The macro handles creating the isolated environment and rolling back any changes after each test completes, so you don't need to write explicit commit or rollback calls in your test functions anymore.

I'm building a CLI tool to parse log files, and I keep trying to run my integration tests against the database. What's the difference between using a manual transaction block in my test code versus just adding the #[sqlx::test] attribute?

The main distinction is that without #[sqlx::test], you have full control but must manually wrap every test function in a BeginBlock, execute your queries, and call CommitBlock before returning. If you forget the rollback or commit, tests can leak state into each other. With #[sqlx::test], sqlx automatically handles the BeginBlock/CommitBlock lifecycle for each #[cfg(test)] module, ensuring isolation without boilerplate.
Ah okay so I just need to add that attribute? But I thought it still required me to write explicit migration code in my tests?

Not exactly. With #[sqlx::test], you don't manually run migrations inside your test functions, but sqlx does require a separate setup function that runs once before any tests execute to apply schema changes. You can define this as a standalone #[cfg(test)] fn called setup() which uses sqlx::migrate::run to load your migrations from the migrations directory. The actual test cases then just run against the migrated database.
Wait so if I have multiple tests and they all try to insert into the same table, will they affect each other? Is there any isolation?

Yes, #[sqlx::test] provides automatic per-test transaction isolation. Each test runs in its own isolated connection with its own transaction scope. When a test finishes, the transaction is rolled back automatically unless you explicitly mark it as stable using the .stable() method on your query builder result, which prevents rollback if needed.
So I can just write normal assertions and not worry about cleanup? Like calling drop or anything?

Exactly. You don't need to call drop or manually manage connections in your test functions when #[sqlx::test] is active. The framework manages the connection pool and transaction boundaries for you, so your test code looks like normal application logic without worrying about resource cleanup.
